coredata.dc:title: Magnetic nanoparticles embedded with graphene quantum dots and multiwalled carbon nanotubes as a sensing platform for electrochemical detection of progesterone
coredata.dc:creator: Arvand, Majid
Hemmati, Shiva
coredata.dc:description: Results of this paper related to fabrication of a simple, reproducible, stable and sensitive electrochemical sensing platform of progesterone (P4) based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) nanocomposite. GQDs, Fe3O4 nanoparticles and functionalized multi–walled carbon nanotubes (f–MWCNTs) modified glassy carbon electrode (Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE) were constructed and the electrocatalytic properties of the modified electrode toward the oxidation of P4 were analyzed. GQDs with the size of about 15nm were prepared by a facile and low expense bottom–up method by carbonization of citric acid and dispersing the carbonized products into alkaline solution. The characterization of the sensor was studied by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X–ray diffraction (XRD), UV–vis spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT–IR) and voltammetry techniques. Electrochemical studies suggested that as–prepared sensor revealed some advantages in terms of high effective surface area, more reactive sites and excellent electrochemical catalytic activity toward the oxidation of P4. After optimization of analytical conditions, the peak currents for P4 were found to vary linearly with its concentrations in the wide range of 0.01–0.5 and 0.5–3.0μM. The estimated detection limit and sensitivity of the electrode were 2.18nM and 16.84μAμM−1, respectively. This sensor exhibited excellent stability, selectivity, sensitivity and reproducibility and could be successfully applied for determination of P4 in human serum samples and pharmaceutical products with excellent recoveries and without the interferences of coexisting substances.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Magnetite nanoparticles
Carbon nanotubes
Nanocomposite
Electrochemical sensor
Progesterone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cyclic voltammetry characterization of modified electrodes
Optimization of f–MWCNTs to Fe3O4@GQD mass ratio
Determination of P4 in biological fluids and pharmaceutical samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram for the synthesis of GQDs.

(A) UV–vis spectra of CA and GQDs, (B) FT–IR spectra of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@GQDs, (C) XRD patterns of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@GQDs, (D) and (E) TEM images of GQDs, (F) TEM image of Fe3O4@GQDs (inset: schematic representation of the structures of the Fe3O4@GQDs).
Cyclic voltammograms of the bare (a) GCE, (b) f–MWCNTs/GCE, (c) Fe3O4/f–MWCNTs/GCE and (d) Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE in 5.0mMK3[Fe(CN)6] containing 0.1M KCl; Scan rate: 0.1Vs−1.

Cyclic voltammograms of P4 (100μM) in PBS (pH=7.0) 0.1M at (a) bare GCE, (b) f–MWCNTs/GCE, (c) Fe3O4/f–MWCNTs/GCE, (d) Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE in the absence of P4 and (e) in the presence of P4; Scan rate: 0.1Vs−1.

(A) Cyclic voltammograms for Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE in 0.1M PBS containing 50μM P4 at different pH over the range of 3.0–9.0; (B) dependence of anodic peak potential and (C) anodic peak current as a function of solution pH.

(A) LSVs for Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE in PBS (pH 7.0) containing 25μM of P4 with scan rates as 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, 0.1, 0.12, 0.14, 0.16Vs−1, respectively. (B) the linear relationship of the anodic peak current vs. scan rate, (C) Tafel plot derived from the current potential curve recorded at scan rate 0.02Vs−1, (D) dependence of the anodic peak potential on the logarithmic scan rate.
(A) DPVs obtained at the Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE for P4 (PBS, pH=7.0) at different concentrations from 0.01 to 3.0μM; (B) the calibration curve of P4.

Amperometric response of Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE to the sequential addition of 0.005μmol P4, 0.05μmolK+, Fe2+, Na+, NH4 +, Mg2+, SO4 2−, Ca2+, Glu, Gly, Cys, Ala, AA and UA into the 25mL stirring 0.1M PBS (pH 7.0) at an applied potential of 0.9V.
Comparison of some characteristics of this modified electrode and other modified electrodes previously reported for P4 detection.
Results of P4 determination in serum samples by DPV compared with ECL method (n =3).

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para:
x =
x ¯
± sx and sx denotes standard deviation.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Tabulated F-value for 2, 2° of freedom at P value of 0.05 is 19.00.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Summary of P4 determination in the commercial ampoules by DPV method (n =4).

head.ce:title: Magnetic nanoparticles embedded with graphene quantum dots and multiwalled carbon nanotubes as a sensing platform for electrochemical detection of progesterone

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electroanalytical Chemistry Laboratory, Faculty of Science, University of Guilan, Namjoo Street, P.O. Box: 1914, Rasht, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Results of this paper related to fabrication of a simple, reproducible, stable and sensitive electrochemical sensing platform of progesterone (P4) based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) nanocomposite. GQDs, Fe3O4 nanoparticles and functionalized multi–walled carbon nanotubes (f–MWCNTs) modified glassy carbon electrode (Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE) were constructed and the electrocatalytic properties of the modified electrode toward the oxidation of P4 were analyzed. GQDs with the size of about 15nm were prepared by a facile and low expense bottom–up method by carbonization of citric acid and dispersing the carbonized products into alkaline solution. The characterization of the sensor was studied by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X–ray diffraction (XRD), UV–vis spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT–IR) and voltammetry techniques. Electrochemical studies suggested that as–prepared sensor revealed some advantages in terms of high effective surface area, more reactive sites and excellent electrochemical catalytic activity toward the oxidation of P4. After optimization of analytical conditions, the peak currents for P4 were found to vary linearly with its concentrations in the wide range of 0.01–0.5 and 0.5–3.0μM. The estimated detection limit and sensitivity of the electrode were 2.18nM and 16.84μAμM−1, respectively. This sensor exhibited excellent stability, selectivity, sensitivity and reproducibility and could be successfully applied for determination of P4 in human serum samples and pharmaceutical products with excellent recoveries and without the interferences of coexisting substances.

ce:section.ce:para: Sensitive, effective and rapid detection of specific biomolecules is critical in medical diagnosis, biological engineering and environmental protection [1]. In the last two decades with the highly developed nanotechniques, various types of nanomaterials including nanoparticles [2,3], carbon nanotubes [4,5], quantum dots [6,7], and electroactive component encapsulated nanoparticles [8,9] have been grabbing scientists’ attention for applications in sensors, biocatalysis, electronics and medicines. The unique chemical and physical properties of nanomaterials and their enormous advantages, such as large surface to volume ratio, electrocatalysis and active surface sites, make them extremely suitable for designing new sensing devices, especially electrochemical sensors and biosensors [10]. Among all nanostructures, quantum dots are one of the best candidates which are superior in terms of their excellent properties [11,12].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a kind of 0D material with characteristics derived from both graphene and carbon dots (CDs), which can be regarded as incredibly small pieces of graphene [13–17] which are one, two or few–layered graphene sheets with lateral dimensions smaller than 100nm [11,14,18–20]. By converting 2D graphene sheets into 0D GQDs, the GQDs exhibit new phenomena due to quantum confinement and edge effects, which are similar to CDs [15,20]. Their novel properties, such as remarkable conductivity, high surface–to–volume ratio, stable luminescence, good biocompatibility, and low toxicity, result in high performance sensors based on GQDs [21–24]. As compared to other carbon nanomaterials, such as graphene and carbon nanotube, GQDs possess larger number of carboxyl groups, much smaller volumes, and have good electrical conductivity. Therefore, it is considered as an excellent candidate for construction of nanoscaled, sensitive, stable and cost–effective electrochemical sensor [13,14,20,25,26].

As an important magnetic material, magnetite (Fe3O4) nanoparticles (MNPs) have attracted an increasing interest for application in sensors, which arising from their high surface area, high biocompatibility, low toxicity, easy preparation, and high adsorption ability. Furthermore, Fe3O4 nanoparticles have a unique ability to promote fast electron transfer between electrode and the active site of the redox reaction [27,28]. However, pure magnetite nanoparticles may not be very stable over time because they are very likely to aggregate to reduce the energy associated with ratio of surface area to volume and the strong dipole–dipole attraction between particles [29–31]. To overcome these limitations, various approaches are used to change the surface of Fe3O4 NPs via the loading of other materials during or after the synthesis process [32,33].

Nowadays, carbon nanotubes mainly multi–walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) have enjoyed widespread attention in the task of sensors and biosensors, owing to their high surface area, high electrical conductivity, good stability, unique electronic and nano-structural properties. It is also known that the surface of the electrode became more porous after adding MWCNTs [34–37]. In addition, Van der Waals and chemical interactions between MWCNTs and graphene materials and electrostatic interactions between MWCNTs and magnetic nanoparticles, resulting in more stable and conductive platform at the surface of modified electrode [38,39].

One of steroid hormones derived from cholesterol with a vital role in human and animal health is progesterone. Progesterone (4–pregnone–3,20 dione, P4) is a non–saturated α,β ketone that is important in the stabilization and maintenance of pregnancy in mammals, acting in the synthetic route of various biologically active steroids [40,42] and playing a role in regulating the sequence of the ovulatory cycle by controlling other hormones [43–45]. Also in the pharmaceutical industry, P4 is the active principle of many drugs used in hormone replacement therapies [46]. An imbalance of P4 in the human organism can cause malformations of the reproductive system and also infertility problems. Therefore, P4 is an ideal candidate as a marker for early diagnosis of pregnancy [41,44,47].

Various techniques have been employed for P4 determination, consist of high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and liquid chromatography (LC) coupled with different detectors (DAD [48], UV [40,49], MS [50], and DCE [51]), time–resolved fluoroimmunoassay [52], fluorescent immunoassay [53], and chemiluminescence enzyme immunoassay (CLEIA) [54]. These approaches possess high sensitivity. Nevertheless, most of these methods suffer from poor selectivity, use of unstable and corrosive reagents, sample preparation steps, considerable time delays between sampling and obtaining results, and require expensive equipments which can adversely affect the analytical results [43,55]. Thus, there has been continuous effort to develop reliable and sensitive electrochemical methods, which can permit cost–effective, convenient, rapid and selective determination of P4 in clinical laboratories.

Taking into account the properties of Fe3O4, GQDs and MWCNTs mentioned above, assembling of these nanomaterials generates a versatile 3D hybrid network with synergic and electrocatalytic characteristics of them. Accordingly, the focus of the present paper is the use of Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs nanocomposites based modified electrodes for sensitive detection of P4. The developed sensor has some advantages such as simplicity of electrode preparation, wide linear range, low detection limit, durability, sensitivity and selectivity, and low cost for the determination of P4 in various real samples. Furthermore, the effects of supporting electrolyte, pH, scan rate, accumulation time and potential and interference effect were studied in details. To the best of our knowledge, up to now, no other works in the literatures have been reported on applying the proposed electrode for determination of P4 in human serum samples and pharmaceutical products.

All reagents and solvents were of the highest purity available from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany) and were used without further purification. Progesterone and other hormones were from Aburaihan Pharmaceutical Co. (Tehran, Iran). Citric acid (CA), iron(ІІІ) chloride (FeCl3·6H2O), iron(ІІ) chloride (FeCl2·4H2O) were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich (St. Louis, Missouri, USA). The MWCNTs (>95%) was obtained from Plasma Chem. GmbH (Berlin, Germany). Aqueous solutions were prepared with phosphate buffer solution (PBS) 0.1M in the pH range 3.0–9.0 by mixing solutions of 0.1M Na2HPO4.12H2O and 0.1M NaH2PO4.H2O. The stock solution of P4 was prepared fresh at the time of experiments. All solutions were prepared and diluted using high quality deionized water.

Electrochemical measurements were conducted using the Autolab PGSTAT 30 electrochemical analyzer (Ecochemie BV, Utrecht, the Netherlands) controlled by Nova 1.7 software. GCE was purchased from Azar Electrode Company (Urmia, Iran). All electrochemical studies were performed at 25±1°C. A conventional three–electrode cell was employed, incorporating a modified working electrode, a saturated Ag/AgCl reference electrode, and a platinum wire counter electrode. The pulse amplitude of 0.025V, pulse interval time of 0.5s and scan rate of 0.01Vs−1 over the potential range of 0.5–1.2V vs. Ag/AgCl were selected as optimum instrumental parameters for differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) measurements. All the pH values were measured with a Metrohm pH meter (model 827, Swiss made). A model EM10C–100KV (Zeiss) transmission electron microscope, Philips PW1840 X–ray powder diffractometer and Shimadzu 8900 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Kyoto, Japan) were used to characterize the morphology, size and structure of the nanomaterials. The amperometry analysis was carried out by PalmSens analyzer at the potential of 0.9V under continuous stirring. Electro–chemiluminescence (ECL) method (Roche, Cobas E411) was used as a reference method for P4 determination.

The GQDs were prepared by directly pyrolyzing of CA (Scheme 1 ) [56]. In a typical procedure of GQDs preparation, 2.0g CA was put into a 5.0mL beaker and heated to 200°C using a heating mantle. About 5min later, the CA was liquated. Subsequently, the color of the liquid was changed from colorless to pale yellow, and then orange in 30min, implying the formation of GQDs. The obtained orange liquid for preparing GQDs was added drop by drop into 50mL of 20mgmL−1 NaOH solution, under vigorous stirring. After neutralized to pH 7.0 with NaOH, the aqueous solution of GQDs was obtained.

Fe3O4 nanoparticles were prepared according to the modified Massart method [57] via the co-precipitation of a mixture of FeCl3·6H2O and FeCl2.4H2O. First, 0.809g FeCl3·6H2O was added to 50mL GQDs solution at room temperature under a nitrogen atmosphere with vigorous stirring. Then, temperature was increased to 80°C, and 0.323g of the FeCl2·4H2O was added slowly to the solution containing Fe3+/GQDs which was vigorously stirred. Then, ammonia solution (25wt.%) was added dropwise to adjust the pH of the solution to 10 for the synthesis of Fe3O4@GQDs. The above mixture kept stirring for 30min at the same conditions to get uniform precipitation. After completing the reaction, the black precipitate was collected using an external magnetic field, followed by several washings with deionized water and ethanol then dried at room temperature. Fe3O4 NPs was synthesized using the same procedure but in the absence of GQDs in the synthesis process.

Prior to modification, glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was first polished with alumina powder to mirror-like surface, then rinsed with 1:1 double–distilled water and ethanol in an ultrasonic bath and allowed to dry at room temperature. Functionalized MWCNTs (f–MWCNTs) were used for preparation of the modified electrode. For this purpose, the MWCNTs were purified and opened in concentrated acids. Therefore pristine MWCNTs were purified by refluxing in conc. nitric acid, filtered and washed with double distilled water until pH of filtrate became ∼7 and finally dried.

To fabrication of modified electrode 3mg of f–MWCNTs and 1mg of Fe3O4@GQD (an optimal mass ratio of 3:1 for f–MWCNTs to Fe3O4@GQD) were added into N,N–dimethylformamide (DMF) and this solution was ultrasonicated for several minutes to form a stable black suspension. 10μL of above suspension was dropped directly on the polished surface of GCE and dried in air at room temperature to obtain the Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE. Prior to each measurement, the modified electrodes were regenerated by repetitive scanning in a blank buffer solution to remove P4 from electrode surface and to provide a fresh one for subsequent experiments.

Accumulation step is an important and effective stage to enhance the determination sensitivity. Therefore, accumulation parameters in terms of accumulation potential (E acc) and accumulation time (t acc) could affect the amount of P4 absorbed at the electrode surface, thus the effects of these parameters on anodic peak current were studied by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV). The optimization of accumulation time was performed at open circuit in PBS (pH=7.0) containing 25μM P4 while stirring. Afterwards by keeping optimum t acc at concentration of 25μM, the effect of accumulation potential was investigated.

The standard addition technique was used for the determination of P4 in real samples according to described procedures. Briefly, the human serum samples were obtained from Danesh Pathobiology Laboratory. Each 0.5mL of the serum samples was diluted to 50mL with the selected supporting electrolyte. Then known volume of diluted human serum samples were spiked with different concentrations of P4 without further treatment. The spiked samples of biological fluids completed to the calibration mark with the selected supporting electrolyte, then transferred into the voltammetric cell and the DP voltammograms were recorded.

Commercial pharmaceutical ampoules of P4 were bought from a local pharmacy in Rasht, Iran (Aburaihan Pharmaceutical Co.). For P4 analysis, 0.5mL of each P4 ampoule was transferred into a 50mL volumetric flask and diluted with the selected supporting electrolyte and mixed. Then, known volume of this solution was diluted with the selected supporting electrolyte, and analyzed with standard addition method as described in serum sample analysis.

Fig. 1 (A) shows the UV–vis spectrum of GQDs and citric acid. According to the UV–vis results in Ref. [56], the GQDs show absorption band at about 380nm which is not observed in the UV–vis spectrum of CA.

The chemical structure of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@GQDs was investigated using FT–IR (Fig. 1(B)). As can be seen, the Fe–O band at Fe3O4@GQDs (640cm−1) shifted to a higher wavelength in comparison with Fe3O4 (590cm−1), indicating the bonding of Fe3O4 to COH groups on the GQDs surface [58]. Peaks at 1020cm−1 and 1384cm−1 can be attributed to the CO group stretching vibration and the OH bending vibration respectively. An absorption bond appeared at about 3400cm−1 corresponding to hydroxyl groups on the Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@GQDs surface and a peak at 1610cm−1 corresponded to the vibration of water molecules adsorbed on Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@GQDs. Moreover strong bond at 1610cm−1 can be contributed to the stretching frequencies of CC on the Fe3O4@GQDs surface [59,60]. This resulted us to believe that the GQDs bound to the Fe3O4 particles through, most probably, Fe–O chemical bonds by this method.

The crystalline structure of the synthesized Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@GQDs was characterized by XRD. The XRD spectra of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@GQDs are shown in Fig. 1(C). The presence of the peaks corresponding to (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440) planes at a 2θ of 30, 36, 44, 54, 57, and 63°, respectively, conform the formation of a spinel structure [61]. Furthermore, Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@GQDs have similar diffraction peaks, which indicate that the crystal structure of Fe3O4 was not changed after modification with GQDs.

The morphologies of the GQDs and Fe3O4@GQDs used in this work were characterized using TEM. TEM images of the resulting GQDs (Fig. 1(D) and (E)) show that the size of GQDs ranging from 10 to 20nm. Furthermore the transparency of the GQDs demonstrates that most of the GQDs consisted of few graphene layers. Fig. 1(F) shows the TEM image of the as prepared Fe3O4@GQDs composite. Most probably, as schematically depicted in inset of Fig. 1(F), the Fe3O4 NPs are surrounded by GQDs to form composite particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cyclic voltammetry characterization of modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical sensing characteristics of the GCE, f–MWCNTs/GCE, Fe3O4/f–MWCNTs/GCE and Fe3O4@GQDs/f–MWCNTs/GCE were studied by cyclic voltammetry (CV) with a solution of 5.0mMK3[Fe(CN)6] probe at scan rate of 0.1Vs−1. As seen in Fig. 2 , a pair of reversible redox peaks with separation between the cathodic and anodic peak potentials (ΔE p) of 115.94, 114.24, 97.51, 90.01mV were observed at GCE (curve a), f–MWCNTs/GCE (curve b), Fe3O4/f–MWCNTs/GCE (curve c) and Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE (curve d), respectively. The slight increase in redox peak currents (I p) was observed at Fe3O4/f–MWCNTs/GCE than f–MWCNTs/GCE. On the other hand, when the GCE was modified with Fe3O4@GQDs and f–MWCNTs, the highest redox peak currents and lower background currents were observed (curve d). These results can be attributed to the Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs nanocomposite which exhibits faster electron transfer kinetics and larger electroactive surface area compared to Fe3O4/f–MWCNTs and f–MWCNTs.

In solution containing K3[Fe(CN)6] redox system, the effective surface area of different electrodes was estimated with CV according to the Randles–Sevcik equation For a reversible process [62]:
(1)
I p =(2.69×105) n 3/2 D 1/2 ν 1/2
A
C*
where I p refers to the anodic peak current, n is the total number of electron transferred (n =1), A is the effective surface area of the electrode, D is the diffusion coefficient for K3[Fe(CN)6]=7.6×10−6 cm2 s−1, C* is the concentration of K3[Fe(CN)6] and ν is the scan rate. Based on Eq. (1), the electrochemically effective surface areas (A) of GCE, f–MWCNTs/GCE, Fe3O4/f–MWCNTs/GCE and Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE were calculated as 0.021, 0.032, 0.044 and 0.068cm2, respectively. The results clearly show that Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE can provide facile electron transfer and more binding sites for electroactive substance than Fe3O4/f–MWCNTs/GCE and f–MWCNTs/GCE.

In the present study, the electrochemical oxidation of P4 at the GCE and modified GCEs was investigated by voltammetric method. Cyclic voltammograms obtained in the presence of 100μM P4 (PBS, pH 7.0) at a potential sweep rate of 100mVs−1, at the GCE (Fig. 3 , curve a), f–MWCNTs/GCE (Fig. 3, curve b), Fe3O4/f–MWCNTs/GCE (Fig. 3, curve c) and Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE in the absence (Fig. 3, curve d) and the presence (Fig. 3, curve e) of P4. It is also apparent from curve d that in the absence of P4 no anodic or cathodic peaks were observed and background current appeared to be sufficiently low on Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE.

The CVs demonstrated that the great improvement on the increase of the electrochemical response and positive shift of peak potential at Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE, were ascribed to the satisfactory synergic effect of Fe3O4 and GQD components which was due to the excellent characteristics of the nanocomposite such as good electrical conductivity, high surface area and more electroactive interaction sites that can facilitate the electron transfer and charge exchange between the analyte and the modified electrode surface. Therefore, as expected for a modifier, Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs caused an increase in the sensitivity of the sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of f–MWCNTs to Fe3O4@GQD mass ratio
ce:section.ce:para: The ratio of f–MWCNTs to Fe3O4@GQD in nanocomposite affects on the electro–oxidation responses. Furthermore, to improve the sensitivity of the Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE, the ratios of 7:1, 5:1, 3:1 and 2:1 (w/w) of the MWCNTs to Fe3O4@GQD were used for fabrication of the modified electrode. As shown in Fig. S1(A), the electrochemical performance of Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE was investigated in solution of P4 at a concentration of 50μM in PBS (pH 7.0) at scan rate of 0.1Vs−1. According to Fig. S1(B), the anodic peak current (I pa) of Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE with mass ratio of 3:1 for MWCNTs to Fe3O4@GQD was found to be higher than others, suggesting that Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE with mass ratio of 3:1 exhibits faster electron transfer kinetics and larger electroactive surface area compared to other modified electrodes. Therefore, the ratio of 3:1 (MWCNTs: Fe3O4@GQD) was selected as the optimum ratio in further studies.

The supporting electrolyte solution and pH value of this solution affect on the peak currents and peak potentials of P4. In order to understand the influence of supporting electrolyte on the linear sweep voltammetric behavior of P4, three kinds of supporting electrolyte solutions (phosphate, acetate and citrate buffer) were used. The highest anodic peak current of P4 was observed in phosphate buffer solution which was chosen as working media.

The influence of the solution pH (phosphate buffer) on the electro-oxidation of P4 (50μM) was investigated in the pH range of 3–9 (Fig. 4 (A)). It was observed that the anodic peak current (I pa) reached its maximum value at pH=7.0 (Fig. 4(B)). Therefore, pH 7.0 was chosen as the best pH condition for the following electrochemical measurements which is the physiological pH value.

In this study, it was found that the oxidation peak potential of P4 shifted to less positive potentials with increase the pH of medium. The pH dependence of E pa can be expressed by the linear regression equation as: E pa(V)=−0.05pH+1.2348 (R 2 =0.9893) (Fig. 4(C)), indicating that the proton is involved in the electrochemical reaction of P4 on Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE. The calculated slope of 0.05V pH−1 is close to the theoretical value of 0.0586V pH−1 which indicates that the number of electrons and protons in the electrochemical oxidation of P4 at the proposed electrode is equal according to Nernst equation [63].

The influence of scan rate on P4 oxidation at the Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE was analyzed by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV). Fig. 5 (A) shows the LSV curves of Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE electrode in 0.1M PBS solution (pH 7.0) containing 25μM analyte at scan rates over the range of 0.02–0.16Vs−1. It is evident that the oxidation peak currents are enhanced as the scan rate increase. As illustrated in Fig. 5(B) the linear regression equation can be expressed as I pa(μA)=609.28 v (Vs−1)+4.4839 (R 2 =0.991). This behavior suggests that the oxidation process of P4 follows adsorptive–controlled mechanism at the Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE [64]. In addition, there was a linear relation between logI pa and logν, corresponding to the following equation: logI pa(μA)=1.2041logν (Vs−1)−3.1894 (R 2 =0.982). The slope of 1.2 indicates that the electrode process is almost absolutely controlled by surface adsorption [65].

A plot of E vs. logI (Fig. 5(C)), known as a Tafel plot, is a useful device for evaluating kinetic parameters. The Tafel slope was determined using the following equation for the anodic reactions [66]:
(2)
E = log
I 0
+ [
2.303 R T
( 1 − α ) n F
] log I
where I 0 and α are the exchange current and the transfer coefficient of the catalytic oxidation of P4, respectively. The Tafel plot derived from the rising part of a voltammogram under quasistatic conditions for the oxidation of 25μM P4 at a scan rate of 0.02Vs−1. A Tafel slope of 0.106V decade−1 is obtained indicating a charge transfer coefficient of 0.49 of the catalytic reaction. It can be seen that the value of α is quite close to its theoretical value. Furthermore, it can be noted that the oxidation peak potential (E pa) shifts positively with the increase of scan rate (Fig. 5(D)), which confirms the irreversibility of the oxidation process. The linear regression equation of the E pa versus the logarithms of scan rates is expressed as: E pa(V)=0.1094logv (V s−1)+0.9847 (R 2 =0.9948). For an irreversible electrode process, Laviron equation can be expressed as [67]:
(3)
E
p a
=
E
0 ′
+
2.3 R T
(
1 − α
)
n F
log ν
where R, F and T have their usual meanings, n is the number of electron transferred and α is the electron transfer coefficient. Thus the number of electrons (n) involved during P4 oxidation in PBS was calculated to be 1at Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE.

The surface concentration of electroactive species, Γ, can be estimated from the slope of the linear relation of peak current as a function of the scan rate, as represented by Eq. (4) [68]:
(4)
I p
=
n 2
F 2
A Γ ν
4 R T
where n, A, Γ and ν represent the number of electrons involved in the reaction, the electrode surface area, the surface coverage of the redox species (μmolcm−2) and the potential scan rate, respectively. From the slope of anodic peak currents vs. scan rate (Fig. 5(B)), the calculated surface concentration of P4 is 2.08×10−2 μmolcm−2 for n =1.

Since the oxidation process of P4 follows adsorptive-controlled mechanism at the Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE, accumulation step is an important and effective way to enhance the determination sensitivity. Therefore, it was important to fix the accumulation potential (E acc) and accumulation time (t acc) when adsorption studies were undertaken. Both conditions could affect the amount of adsorption of P4 at the electrode, thus the effect of accumulation potential and time on peak current response was studied by LSV. The optimization of accumulation time was performed at open circuit in 25μM P4 solution for 10–220s while stirring. As shown in Fig. S2(A), the oxidation peak current of P4 gradually increased with increasing accumulation time from 10 to 220s, and the maximum current response was obtained at 140s and for longer accumulation times, the peak currents were stabilized. This may be caused by the fact that the adsorption of P4 on the Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE surface becomes saturated and showing equilibrium between the concentration of the substance in solution and that on the surface of the modified electrode. Therefore, an accumulation time of 140s was used for each measurement of P4.Keeping t acc =140s at a concentration of 25μM, the accumulation potential was investigated in the range of 0.1–0.7V. As can be seen in Fig. S2(B),The peak current reached its maximum at an accumulation potential of 0.6V. Therefore 140s and 0.6V were used as the optimal accumulation conditions for each measurement of P4.

As a highly sensitive electrochemical method, the DPV has much higher current sensitivity and better resolution compared to CV. Therefore, DPV method is used for determination of P4 with the Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE. Under the optimum conditions, with the increase of P4 concentrations, the DPV oxidation peaks increased gradually (Fig. 6 (A)). The results demonstrated that anodic peak current were proportional to two ranges of P4 concentration intervals (Fig. 6(B)), one is 0.01–0.5μM and the other is 0.5–3.0μM. The corresponding linear function of the prior was I pa(μA)=16.841C (μM)+0.1816, with a correlation coefficient of R 2 =0.9968; The latter was I pa(μA)=12.533C (μM)+2.9306, R 2 =0.9927. The reason for two linear regions may be as follows: At higher concentration than 0.5μM, saturation took place and this defined the decrease in the current in the interval from 0.5 to 3.0μM or P4 transport mechanism altered. The difference in the slopes for the calibration curves is probably due to the different activity of the electrode surface with low and high concentration of the P4. In the lower P4 concentration, due to a high number of active sites (in relation to the total number of the analyte molecules), the slope of the first calibration curve is high. While in the higher P4 concentration, due to decreasing active sites (in relation to the total number of analyte molecules, mainly at the surface of the electrode), therefore the slope of the second calibration decreased [69].

The limit of detection (LOD) of the proposed modified electrode, which is defined as the concentration of the sample yielding a signal equal to the blank signal three times of its standard deviation, was found to be 2.18nM (n =6). This range of detection and LOD are suitable for sensing P4 concentration in human body (3–900nM) [70]. A comparison between the analytical performance of the present modified electrode and some previous literature electrodes for the determination of P4 is given in Table 1 .

For validation of the proposed method, various parameters such as repeatability, reproducibility, stability of the analysis were evaluated. The repeatability of one Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE was examined by the detection of 0.5μM P4 in PBS (pH 7.0) for five successive determinations. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of the results is 1.45% for P4, indicating that the Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE has good repeatability. In addition, under the same procedure, five Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GC electrodes were prepared independently and the fabrication reproducibility was also estimated. The RSD is 3.11% for the peak current measured in 0.5μM P4 in PBS (pH 7.0), which demonstrates the reliability of the fabrication procedure.

In addition, the storage stability of the designed sensor was investigated by DPV method. For detection of 0.5μM P4, there was no significant decrease in current response in the first 3 weeks. Only about 6% decrease occurred after 4 weeks. An 85% of its initial response was retained after 6 weeks. Therefore, the stability of the proposed electrode was good enough for continual operations.

In order to further extend the selectivity of the proposed method, verification of the matrix effect in the detection of 0.2μM P4 was evaluated by introducing some possible interfering molecules and ions which are found in biological fluids and pharmaceuticals samples such as glucose (Glu), alanine (Ala), glycine (Gly), cysteine (Cys), ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA) and some ions by amperometry technique. These species were consecutively added into continuously stirred 0.1M PBS (pH 7.0) at an applied potential of 0.9V. It can be seen from Fig. 7 that there is nearly no current response for all the interferents, compared to the response for 0.2μM P4. First 0.2μM P4 was added into the buffer solution. After addition of the interfering species, 0.2μM P4 was again added into the solution. It was observed that the current response still reached the similar value for the first addition of P4, despite the previous additions of the interferents which shows the selectivity of the as-prepared modified electrode toward P4 determination.

The influence of the foreign species on the determination of P4 was also investigated by DPV method; if peak current change is no more than ±10% when foreign substance is added, we assume that no interference occurs. The DPV results indicated that common cations and anions, such as K+, SO4 2−, NO3 −, NH4 +, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe2+ and Cl− had no interference on P4 determination by the proposed electrode even when they were present in 500–fold excess over P4. Furthermore, the results showed tolerance limit of 500–fold of glucose, alanine and glycine, 100–fold for l–dopa and hydrocortisone, 25–fold for ascorbic acid and uric acid, 10–fold for estradiol. In agreement with the results obtained with amperometric method, DPV results showed the present modified electrode was highly selective towards the determination of P4 in the presence of common interferents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of P4 in biological fluids and pharmaceutical samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the ability of the modified electrode for the analysis of real samples and to investigate the possibility of interfering matrix effects, P4 concentration was determined in P4 ampoules and human serum samples by standard addition method and measuring DPV responses. In this regard, the samples were prepared as described in the sample preparation section. The results are presented in Tables 2 and 3. The obtained results for determination of P4 in human serum sample using the suggested sensor and electro–chemiluminescence (ECL) method are exhibited in Table 2 . Applying the statistical calculations (paired t–test and F–test) to the results obtained by two methods, it was found that all results were in good agreement at the 95% confidence level with an acceptable range of error and no significant difference.

According to the results of pharmaceutical samples (Table 3 ), the obtained recoveries and standard deviations were satisfactory and acceptable. Thus these results confirm the good precision and accuracy of the proposed method and indicating that this sensor had great potential in the practical sample analysis and is free from interference of important common oxidizable species found in biological fluids and pharmaceutical samples.

In this study, we exhibited a rapid and sensitive method for the detection of P4. Compared with the Fe3O4/f–MWCNTs/GCE, f–MWCNTs/GCE and GCE, the peak current responses of P4 were improved significantly and the oxidation peak shifted toward less positive potential at the Fe3O4@GQD/f–MWCNTs/GCE surface. The remarkable signal enlargements can be attributed to the special properties of GQDs and the synergistic interactions between the GQDs and Fe3O4 NPs. The preparation of this modified electrode is so easy and inexpensive. Moreover, the use of this electrode for electrochemical measurements of P4 has some advantages, such as fast electron transfer reaction, high stability, selectivity, repeatability and reproducibility as well as low LOD and wide linear range, and surface renewal. Thereby has promising applications for the determination of trace amounts of P4 in biological fluids and pharmaceutical samples without complex pretreatments and interference from common species present in real samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the post-graduate office of Guilan University for the support of this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.07.066.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: M. Arvand received his BSc in chemistry from Guilan University (1996), MSc degree in applied chemistry from Tehran University (1998), and PhD degree in analytical chemistry from Tarbiat Modarres University (2003). He joined the faculty of Guilan University in 2003. His main area of interest at present is electroanalytical chemistry.

Shiva Hemmati received her BSc degree in 2010 from Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, Iran; MSc degree in 2013 from Iran University of Science & Technology, Tehran, Iran. At present, she is a PhD student in electroanalytical chemistry department of Guilan University, Rasht, Iran.
